extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) holds on every reachable execution.

Key facts:
1. opted_out is set true only by [t_optout] on `optout`, and NO transition ever sets opted_out back to false. So once opt-out occurs, opted_out is permanently true.
2. sent is set to true only by [t_send_allowed] on `send_attempt`, whose guard is `not opted_out`. Thus sent can only become true while opted_out is false; that transition does not modify opted_out, so the resulting state has opted_out=false, sent=true — which satisfies the invariant.
3. [t_optout] itself sets sent := false in the same step it sets opted_out := true, so the post-opt-out state has sent=false.
4. After opted_out is true: the only sent-setter [t_send_allowed] is permanently disabled (guard not opted_out can never hold again). Every `send_attempt` instead fires [t_send_blocked], setting sent := false. Every key_0/key_1/key_2 transition (all advance and reset rules of the combination lock) sets sent := false. The lock stages only mutate `stage`; they never touch opted_out and never set sent := true.

Therefore in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false. The combination-lock stage logic is irrelevant to the safety property: there is no path — at any stage value 0..8, in any order — by which a send can be recorded (sent=true) while opted_out is true. No counterexample exists within 11 events (or any length).
